exemplar.title:
How does the length of a stretched spring affect the velocity of a
longitudinal wave travelling along it?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How does the length of a stretched spring affect the velocity of a
longitudinal wave travelling along it?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables (raw and processed) include units and stated uncertainties, and the graph features error bars.
Uncertainties are quantified and propagated (percentage for velocity, absolute for ââ) with an attempt at linearisation.
Processing steps (averages, velocity calculation from Îs/Ît, linearisation) are carried out demonstrating methodical work.
weaknesses: Headings and units are inconsistent (e.g. velocity values differ by orders of magnitude; misprints in unit notation).
Uncertainty propagation contains algebraic errors and unjustified rounding of Îv.
Systematic uncertainties (spring constant, calibration) are ignored and some calculations yield implausible magnitudes.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows clear evidence of data recording, processing, and consideration of uncertainties, but frequent formatting errors, calculation inconsistencies, and omissions place it in the 3â4 band. A score of 3 reflects these strengths and significant weaknesses.

strengths: The research question is embedded in a specific physical context (a stretched spring system), clearly referencing governing equations and key variables.
Methodology description (equipment list, diagram, control table, trial procedures) is sufficiently detailed to allow reproduction with minor ambiguities.
Methodological considerations are explained, including control of amplitude, spring mass, stand height, and propagation of measurement uncertainties.
weaknesses: Conceptual error in describing how spring extension affects linear density may misdirect interpretation.
The hypothesis states direct proportionality when theory predicts a squareâroot relationship (vâââ).
Some controls (e.g. ambient temperature, exact tension value) and derivations (linking F=kx to tension T) are not fully elaborated.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this criterion solidly in the 5â6 band, with only minor ambiguities preventing a perfect score. The detailed equipment list, clear context, and explained methodological considerations justify a score of 5 out of 6.

strengths: The conclusion correctly identifies a positive correlation and refers to the graphâs slope.
There is an attempt to relate findings to the waveâspeed formula and Hookeâs law, with some reference to textbook values.
weaknesses: The conclusion is not fully consistent with the hypothesis or the (flawed) numerical analysis.
Notation is inconsistent (undefined symbols such as r), and the discussion of why the graph does not pass through the origin is superficial.
Comparison to accepted scientific context is brief and does not reconcile experimental deviations quantitatively.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the conclusion shows some link to analysis and theory, inconsistencies in symbols and lack of depth in scientific contextualisation place it at the lower end of the 3â4 band. A score of 2 acknowledges the attempt while reflecting the limited consistency and support.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (reactionâtime error, damping, calibration issues) are identified.
Realistic improvements (highâspeed camera, better calibration) are suggested.
weaknesses: The impact of each weakness on results is described only qualitatively, with no quantitative estimation.
Improvements lack explanation of how they would be implemented or how much they would enhance accuracy.
The evaluation is brief and does not fully explain the relative importance of each suggested change.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation identifies relevant limitations and proposes realistic improvements, yet the lack of quantitative impact analysis and detailed explanation limits its depth. This situates it at the lower end of the 1â2 band, warranting a score of 1.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated, linked to longitudinal-wave dynamics, and anchored to a very specific physical system (a stretched spring between two clamp stands).  The introduction explains why this relationship is interesting, cites the governing equations and identifies the key variables.  Because the reader knows exactly what type of spring, what range of extensions, and what physical effect is being probed, the context is both **specific and appropriate**.  Minor gaps (e.g. spring constant not given at this stage) do not prevent understanding of the experimental setting, so a mark in the 5-6 band is warranted; the detail is sufficient for 5 rather than the very top of the band.

The student quantifies instrument uncertainties, propagates them (percentage for velocity, absolute for âlox) and places error bars on the graph.  Yet the propagation contains algebraic errors, uncertainty on velocity is rounded to a constant Â±0.05 m sâ»Â¹ without justification, and systematic uncertainties (e.g. spring constant) are ignored.  Consequently there is **evidence of consideration with significant omissions**, meriting 3 marks in the 3-4 band.

Methodological considerations are not merely listed; the student **explains** why amplitude, spring mass, and stand height matter and how they are controlled.  Uncertainties for ruler and stopwatch are calculated and propagated; safety, ethical and environmental issues are addressed.  While some controls (e.g. ambient temperature, exact tension value) are omitted, the discussion shows clear understanding of collecting **relevant and sufficient** data, fitting the 5-6 descriptor; 5 marks represent solid explanation without being exhaustive.

Specific weaknesses are identified, including reaction-time error, finger contact damping, and possible calibration issues, but the impact on results is stated qualitatively (e.g. âaffecting the y-interceptâ) rather than quantitatively.  This satisfies the descriptor **âdescribes specific methodological weaknessesâ**, earning 3 marks within the 3-4 band.

The student supplies a step-by-step method, complete equipment list, photographs, a labelled diagram and control-variable table.  Distances, number of trials (five), extensions and timing procedure are spelled out so that another investigator could reconstruct the set-up.  Some ambiguity remains (how the finger contact point is placed; no calibration of spring constant), but overall the description **allows reproduction**; hence placement in the 5-6 band, at 5 marks because of the small residual ambiguities.

Averages are calculated correctly, velocities derived through v = Îs/Ît, and linearisation (âlox) attempted.  Nonetheless, velocity magnitudes conflict with the time data (28 m sâ»Â¹ over 0.1 m gives implausible numbers) and the uncertainty propagation mixes units.  These **inaccuracies and inconsistencies** indicate the processing is adequate but flawed, fitting the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

Improvements such as using a high-speed camera and better calibration are realistic and directly address identified weaknesses, yet their explanation is brief and lacks detail on how they would quantitatively enhance accuracy.  Therefore improvements are **described, not fully explained**, placing the work in the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

Raw and processed tables are present with units and stated uncertainties, and the graph includes error bars.  However, headings are sometimes truncated, units are inconsistent (velocity table uses values such as 28.4 m sâ»Â¹ but later graph shows 0.10 m sâ»Â¹), and some symbols (ms-1) are misprinted.  This means communication is **clear OR precise, but not both**, placing it in the 3-4 band; 3 marks reflect the frequent minor formatting and unit issues.

The student links findings to the standard wave-speed formula and to Hookeâs law, and cites a textbook.  However, the comparison is brief and does not fully reconcile why the experimental line fails to pass through the origin or why velocity magnitudes differ from theoretical expectations.  This provides **some relevant comparison** but lacks depth, warranting 3 marks in the 3-4 band.

The conclusion correctly states the observed positive correlation and refers to the graph and gradient, but the sign of the relationship conflicts with the hypothesis wording and the discussion of linear density.  Support from numerical analysis is limited and partly inconsistent due to calculation issues.  Thus the conclusion is **described but not fully consistent** with the analysis, matching 3-4 band; 3 marks are appropriate.

annotations.comment: Clear and focused research question situates the investigation within the specific context of a stretched spring system, enabling reproducibility and guiding the experimental design effectively.

The assertion that increasing spring length raises mass per unit length is incorrect; under constant mass, extending the spring reduces linear density. This conceptual error could misdirect data interpretation.
Clarify the relationship between tension, extension, and linear density by defining each variable explicitly and connecting the theoretical model to the physical interpretation before hypothesising.
The breakdown of variables in the elastic force equation, including units, supports clarity and helps to contextualise Hookeâs Law within the experimental framework.
The description of longitudinal wave propagation contains a minor redundancy and could be condensed; consider focusing on the relevance of compressions and rarefactions to wave velocity measurements.
Introduce definitions for $$T$$ and $$\rho$$ immediately following the equation to ensure the reader grasps the physical meaning of each term without referring to external sources.
The transition from Hookeâs Law description to its application is abrupt; a concise derivation linking $$F= kx$$ to tension $$T$$ in the spring would strengthen the background.
Using hand-counting for oscillations introduces significant human error; consider describing how to improve time resolution (e.g. multiple counters or digital sensors) in the methodology.
Only one control variable is specified. To meet rigorous reproducibility, list and justify all relevant controls (e.g. ambient temperature, spring pre-tension) in this table.
The inclusion of an apparatus photograph alongside a clear equipment list with quantities and symbols aids in visualising the setup and supports experimental reproducibility.
Prefix the velocity equation with a statement of its assumptions (small amplitude, one-dimensional wave) to clarify the conditions under which $$v=\frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$$ applies.

The hypothesis claims direct proportionality between spring length and velocity, but theory predicts $$v\propto\sqrt{\ell}$$. Revising the hypothesis to reflect the square-root relationship will align it with the model.
Specify the independent and dependent variables in the hypothesis statement (e.g., $$x=\sqrt{\ell}$$ vs. $$y=v$$) to make clear on which axes the expected linear relationship will be tested.
The sequence of equations is inconsistent: the transition to $$v=\tfrac{\sqrt{k}}{\sqrt{m}}\sqrt{lx}$$ mixes variables without defining $$x$$ clearly. Streamline each step and label all terms.
The independent variable table comprehensively details measurement methods, ranges, and rationales, enhancing reproducibility and demonstrating sound methodological planning.
Include the numeric values of the springâs mass $$m$$ and spring constant $$k$$ in the variables list to ensure readers can follow calculations without searching back through the method.
The uncertainty notation uses $$\Delta x$$ for both length and time; labeling the reaction-time uncertainty as $$\Delta t$$ would avoid confusion between positional and temporal measurements.
Instead of summing uncertainties linearly, apply the root-sum-square method for independent measurements to better represent combined measurement error.
The reported ruler uncertainty of $$\pm0.3\text{ cm}$$ conflicts with earlier $$\pm0.001\text{ m}$$. Consistency in uncertainty values is vital; reconcile these figures or explain the discrepancy.
The table heading lists time uncertainty as $$\pm0.005\text{ s}$$, whereas the text cites $$\pm0.14\text{ s}$$. This inconsistency undermines reliability; correct one value to maintain coherence.
Velocity values (e.g. 28.4 m/s at 0.10 m/3.53 s) are three orders of magnitude too large; this suggests miscalculation. Recompute $$v=\Delta s/\Delta t$$ to ensure accurate magnitudes.
Add axis labels with units and error bars on the processed graph to visually convey measurement uncertainty and improve interpretability.

The graphical analysis section states a line âdoes not pass through the originâ without quantifying the intercept deviation. Including the $$y$$-intercept value and its uncertainty would strengthen the analysis.
The conclusion repeats a formula $$\nu=\frac{\sqrt{r}}{\sqrt{\rho}}$$ where $$r$$ is undefined. Ensure consistency in notation and define all symbols in the concluding section.
To explain the impact of finger damping quantitatively, estimate how contact force alters wave amplitude or speed, perhaps via a simple damping coefficient, and discuss its effect on the gradient.

The evaluation clearly articulates methodological strengths, such as multiple trials and controlled variables, demonstrating reflective awareness of reproducibility and reliability in experimental design.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
